key_insight: Realistic piezoelectrically actuated surface deformations can be designed to generate traveling, hybrid, and standing waves that significantly reduce turbulent drag, with spanwise waves performing best.
engineering_relevance: This work shows a practical path to passive-active flow control by using FEM-predicted actuator/sheet deformations as DNS wall boundary conditions for drag reduction in channels.
method: Finite element analysis of a piezoelectric actuator-Aluminum sheet system across 119–543 Hz and 250–500 V.
Direct numerical simulation of turbulent half-channel flow at Reτ = 200 using the computed wall deformations.
Comparison of upstream, downstream, and spanwise wave directions to quantify drag-reduction performance.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports maximum drag reductions of 1.6%, 5.4%, and 27.6% for upstream, downstream, and spanwise waves, respectively, over the tested actuation range.
watch_item: Performance is demonstrated at Reτ = 200 in simulations, so real-world effectiveness may depend on actuator durability, control bandwidth, and scaling to higher Reynolds numbers.
paper_title: Direct numerical simulations of turbulent drag reduction via piezoelectric actuation
